title: Needle-felted duck
subtitle: A farmyard mallard in white or buff merino with a yellow beak
excerpt: Felt a plump duck body in white or buff merino with a flat yellow beak and webbed feet suggested by a small flap of orange. The finished duck stands about 8 cm long and suits a rustic shelf arrangement or a farmyard scene.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from British needle-felting workshop traditions. Duck forms are a common beginner project in farmyard-animal felting series.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to compress and sculpt wool fibres without water. Each stab draws the barbs through the fibre, tangling the scales until the mass holds its shape.
The stage in wet or needle felting when fibres have been worked enough to bind together firmly and resist pulling apart. A fully needled piece feels solid and springs back when pressed.

content.text: This duck is worked in a single sitting using a teardrop body shape and a separate round head. The beak is a flattened orange or yellow cone. White merino gives a domestic farmyard duck; buff or light brown merino gives a female mallard. A duck at this scale works well for Christmas tree decorations, table centrepieces, and children's room displays.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees into the foam pad only. Wear a finger guard on the hand holding the work.

items.name: Merino roving, orange or yellow (beak and feet)

content.text: Fold 12 g of white merino into a teardrop shape, wider at the front and tapering toward the tail. Place on the mat and needle all over with the size-36 needle to compact the core. Rotate through all sides. The body should be about 8 cm long, 5 cm wide at the breast, and 3 cm tall when finished.
Step 2: Shape the head and neck

Roll 4 g of white merino into a compact ball about 3 cm across. Needle it to a round shape on the mat. Position it on top of the front third of the body, slightly forward and up, as a sitting duck holds its head. Bridge the join with a thin wisp of merino and needle smoothly to blend neck and body.
Step 3: Flatten and attach the tail

Pull a thin oval of white merino about 4 cm by 2 cm. Needle it flat on the mat. Attach it to the rear underside of the body, angling it slightly upward to give the characteristic duck tail posture. Needle in from the top surface of the body to lock the tail base firmly.

Roll a small oblong of orange merino about 2 cm long. Needle it flat and wide rather than pointed, since duck beaks are broad and spatula-shaped. Attach it to the front of the head with the broader end face-on and the tip pointing slightly down. Use the size-38 needle for this detail.

Roll two tiny black merino dots about 3 mm each and needle them to the sides of the head at the correct position (halfway up the head, just behind the beak base). For the feet, pull two small fan shapes of orange merino and needle each flat on the mat. Attach them to the underside of the body so the duck appears to stand. The feet give the piece a solid base to display on a shelf.
Once all parts are attached, go over the whole surface with the size-40 needle to smooth any rough patches. A fully worked piece, known in felting as
, feels dense and resistant to pulling apart when pinched at the surface. The
is complete when the surface is smooth and uniform.

items.problem: The duck lists to one side when placed on a surface
items.cause: The feet are not parallel or one is attached at a different angle
items.fix: Detach the lower foot by pulling gently while the felt is still slightly soft. Reposition it so both feet sit flat and parallel, then needle firmly to reattach.

items.problem: The head does not look integrated with the body
items.cause: The neck join is too narrow, creating a visible seam
items.fix: Add a small amount of merino across the neck area and needle it in layers from both the head and body sides. Work until the transition is smooth and no seam is visible.
